ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Acar Awak; closest match is the generic pickled vegetable food product class. Acar Awak is a Nyonya-style spiced pickled vegetable dish with peanuts and sesame seeds.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pickled vegetables) is the closest defensible anchor. No specific Acar Awak entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Acar Awak or Nyonya pickled vegetables.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Acar Awak as an accompaniment is approximately 50 g, equivalent to a small side-dish portion served alongside rice dishes such as Nasi Lemak or Briyani.

nutrient_source.title: Nutrition Facts for Malaysian Pickled Vegetables — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore food composition database entry for Malaysian pickled vegetables, which is the closest approved-source match for Acar Awak. Values for peanut- and sesame-enriched Nyonya-style preparation are consistent with the HPB entry and have been cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central entries for individual ingredients (cucumber, carrot, peanuts, sesame seeds). Saturated fat estimated at approximately 1.1 g per 100 g based on peanut and sesame seed fat fraction data from USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate based on non-starchy mixed pickled vegetables with vinegar
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Acar Awak. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous non-starchy mixed pickled vegetables with vinegar from the University of Sydney GI Database. The primary ingredients are non-starchy vegetables (cucumber, carrot, long beans, cabbage) and peanuts; vinegar in the pickling medium further attenuates the glycemic response. Glycemic load per 50 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (35 × 4.9) / 100 ≈ 1.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (280 mg per 100 g; 140 mg per 50 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 50 g or less and monitor cumulative sodium intake from the pickling liquid and added salt, as a single serving contributes approximately 140 mg sodium toward the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low glycemic load per serving (~1.7)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Acar Awak has a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 1.7 per 50 g serving, making it a suitable accompaniment for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the sugar content in the pickling liquid (4.5 g per 100 g) should be accounted for in total carbohydrate budgeting.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly unsaturated fat from peanuts and sesame seeds; low saturated fat (1.1 g per 100 g); anti-inflammatory spices
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fat profile of Acar Awak is predominantly unsaturated (peanuts and sesame seeds contribute monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats), and the anti-inflammatory spices turmeric and galangal may confer additional cardiovascular benefit; sodium intake should still be monitored in individuals with concurrent hypertension.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory turmeric and galangal; nightshade chili may be a trigger in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Turmeric and galangal in the rempah paste have documented anti-inflammatory properties that may benefit individuals with rheumatoid arthritis, but those sensitive to nightshades should be aware that chili is a key ingredient.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Acar Awak is a traditional Nyonya (Peranakan) pickled vegetable dish widely consumed in Malaysia and Singapore as a refreshing accompaniment to rich rice dishes such as Nasi Lemak, Briyani, and Nyonya laksa. The dish features a medley of crunchy vegetables — most commonly cucumber, carrot, long beans, and cabbage — that are briefly blanched and then marinated in a complex spiced pickling medium. The pickling base is built from a freshly ground rempah (spice paste) comprising dried chilies, shallots, garlic, fresh turmeric, galangal, and lemongrass, which is sautéed in oil before being combined with rice vinegar and sugar to create a tangy, mildly sweet, and aromatic brine. A defining characteristic of Acar Awak that distinguishes it from simpler pickles is the generous incorporation of toasted, coarsely crushed peanuts and toasted sesame seeds, which contribute a rich nutty flavour, textural contrast, and additional protein and healthy unsaturated fats. The dish is typically prepared a day or two in advance to allow the flavours to mature and deepen. Nutritionally, Acar Awak provides approximately 115 kcal per 100 g, with 3.2 g protein, 7.5 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from peanuts and sesame), and 9.8 g carbohydrate. It is a good source of dietary fibre (2.5 g), vitamin A (85 mcg RAE), vitamin C (12 mg), and several B vitamins. The sodium content of 280 mg per 100 g warrants attention for individuals on sodium-restricted diets. Its estimated glycemic index of approximately 35 (low) makes it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is naturally vegetarian, vegan, halal, and gluten-free, though it contains peanuts and sesame seeds as major allergens.
common_user_queries: Is Acar Awak suitable for diabetics?
How many calories in Acar Awak?
What vegetables are in Acar Awak?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from the HPB Singapore food composition database entry for Malaysian pickled vegetables, which is the closest approved-source match. No exact Acar Awak entry exists in any approved primary source. Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions are estimated from USDA SR Legacy ingredient-level data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for non-starchy mixed pickled vegetables with vinegar. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. water_g is set to -1: moisture data for Acar Awak as a composite pickled dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: these are confirmed zero / naturally zero values as Acar Awak is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant foods and vitamin D is naturally zero in this preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 confirmed as no hydrogenated fats are used. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 confirmed as the dish is entirely plant-based. 3. Saturated fat (1.1 g per 100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.2 g per 100 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (2.6 g per 100 g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for peanuts and sesame seeds proportional to their contribution in a standard Acar Awak recipe. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate based on non-starchy mixed pickled vegetables with vinegar from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Acar Awak has been published. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (35 × 4.9 g available carbohydrate per 50 g serving) / 100 ≈ 1.7. 6. FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_03315873 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are close matches only; no exact ontology code exists for Acar Awak.
